displayTitle: How Radiation Hurts You, One DNA Break at a Time
kicker: RAD SAFETY // BIOLOGICAL EFFECTS · ACUTE DOSE

beats.narration: Radiation hurts you by breaking molecules inside your cells. One DNA break, and the cell faces three very different fates.

payload.title: ONE BREAK, THREE FATES
payload.sub: The injury begins at the molecule, not the skin.

beats.narration: A photon rarely hits DNA directly. More often it splits water into free radicals that attack the strand. Then: repair, death, or misrepair.

payload.heading: Where the damage begins and ends
payload.note: MISREPAIR SURVIVES - AND THAT IS THE SEED OF CANCER

beats.narration: Those fates split harm into two families. Cell killing gives deterministic effects with a threshold. Misrepair gives stochastic cancer with no safe threshold.
Acute whole-body dose is the worst case. Below twenty-five rad, nothing. A few hundred brings nausea and marrow failure. Half die near four-fifty.
And it gives no warning at the time. No pain, no sensation. So act on the meter and the incident, never on how it feels.

payload.sub: Delayed reddening or early nausea is an emergency, not a wait-and-see.
